tldr: GO Feature Flag is a self-hosted, OpenFeature-native flag service driven by Git/S3/HTTP-stored configs; Bucket is a managed feature-management SaaS with a hosted UI. Migration trades operational ownership for a managed product and re-creates flags, rules, and SDK wiring inside Bucket.
why_switch: Drop the operational burden of running and scaling a self-hosted flag service
Provide a polished UI for product/PM users to toggle features
Centralize flag governance instead of managing config files in Git/S3
Reduce relay-proxy and SDK operational complexity
Get an integrated feature-management workflow out of the box
what_to_export: All GO Feature Flag config files (YAML/JSON)
Per-flag targeting rules and rollout strategies
Relay proxy configuration and exporter settings
Flag descriptions, owners, and ticket references
Custom retrievers, exporters, or notifiers in use
User attribute schema used for targeting
export_method: GO Feature Flag stores its source of truth in flag configuration files retrieved from Git, S3, HTTP, Kubernetes, or local disk. Collect every config file across every environment — these already contain flags, variants, default rules, and rollout strategies in a portable format. Inventory the relay-proxy configuration, retriever and exporter setup, and any custom notifier or evaluator extensions so they can be migrated or retired. Snapshot all files and infra config at a known commit so you can diff against the post-migration state in Bucket and confirm flag-by-flag parity.
import_method: Bucket does not have a native GO Feature Flag importer, so re-create flags through Bucket's UI or its API. For each GO Feature Flag entry, create a corresponding feature in Bucket with the same key, default, and variants. Translate rule expressions and rollout strategies into Bucket's segmentation and rule model — user/company attributes plus percentage rollouts. Replace the GO Feature Flag OpenFeature provider in services with Bucket's SDK, or with a Bucket OpenFeature provider where available, to keep call sites stable. Update identify calls to pass the user/company context Bucket expects. Run both systems in parallel in staging, validate each flag, then cut traffic and decommission the relay proxy and config retrievers.

concept_mapping.note: Re-create via UI/API; preserve flag keys to minimize code change
Rule semantics differ; re-test edge cases
Polyglot SDKs talk directly to Bucket
Re-route analytics; some sinks may need bridges

gotchas: Self-hosted to SaaS means user data leaves your perimeter — confirm privacy and compliance constraints first
Rule expressions and progressive rollout shapes may not map 1:1; validate complex predicates carefully
High-QPS workloads on Bucket can cost materially more than the free OSS option
Local zero-latency evaluation via the relay proxy is replaced by network calls; benchmark hot paths
Custom retrievers, exporters, and notifiers have no direct SaaS equivalent and must be retired or bridged
OpenFeature compatibility depends on Bucket's provider availability; otherwise call sites change

email.subject: Moving feature flags from GO Feature Flag to Bucket
email.body:
Hi team,

We're planning to migrate from self-hosted GO Feature Flag to Bucket, a managed feature-management platform, over the next 14 days. The motivation is less infrastructure to operate and a better UI for non-engineers.

What changes for you:
- Flag definitions will move out of our Git/S3 config files into the Bucket dashboard.
- Services will swap the GO Feature Flag OpenFeature provider for Bucket's SDK (or its OpenFeature provider where available).
- Relay proxy and config retrievers will be decommissioned after cutover.

We'll run both systems in parallel during the overlap and only retire GO Feature Flag once parity is confirmed in production. Please flag any rule, retriever, or exporter you rely on so we can verify it survives the move.

Thanks,
Your customer
